front: Philosopher: distinguishes between the 'general will' and the 'will of all' in a work opening 'man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains'
work: The Social Contract and the General Will

front: Philosopher: argues those who refuse to join the 'whole body' will be 'forced to be free'
work: The Social Contract and the General Will

front: Philosopher: Mary Astell anticipated this thinker's famous opening line with 'if all men are born free, how is it that all women are born slaves?'
work: The Social Contract and the General Will

front: Work: traces civil society to the first man who fenced off land and said 'This is mine'; contrasts amour de soi with amour-propre
Concept: a form of self-love dependent on how one is perceived by others, contrasted with amour de soi
Work: argues man in the state of nature is happy rather than brutal and differs from beasts via 'perfectibility'; written for an Academy of Dijon contest
Philosopher: the stag hunt scenario, in which hunters defect to pursue a hare, inspired Brian Skyrms's game theory
Work: educational treatise in the style of a Bildungsroman; burned because of the 'Profession of Faith of a Savoyard Vicar'
Philosopher: argued women should be educated for attractiveness and domesticity, prompting Mary Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman
Work: epistolary novel whose title character is a 'New Heloise' taught by Saint-Preux; based on Peter Abelard's story
Philosopher: accused David Hume of plotting against him while Hume sheltered him during the fallout over one of his books
Philosopher: Jacques Derrida used the example of masturbation to illustrate his reworking of this thinker's concept of the supplement in Of Grammatology
Philosopher: Paul de Man analyzed the Marion ribbon theft episode from this thinker's Confessions in his essay 'Excuses' in Allegories of Reading
